content.text: I'll start the spec review process by reading the spec and its dependencies.

content.content:
1	# Spec: wordfreq CLI
2	
3	**Status:** Draft
4	**Owner:** Reid W
5	**Created:** 2026-07-02
6	**Complexity:** LOW
7	**Branch:** N/A (no commits yet in this sandbox)
8	
9	---
10	
11	## Problem
12	
13	There's no quick way to see which words dominate a text document. Doing this
14	by hand, or with a one-off script each time, is repetitive, and a naive word
15	count is dominated by function words (the, a, is, of, ...) that carry no
16	information about what the document is actually about. A small CLI that
17	counts words, strips stopwords, and shows the top N gives an at-a-glance
18	read on any text file's content.
19	
20	## Success Criteria
21	
22	- [ ] Running the tool against a text file prints a ranked list of the
23	      document's most frequent words, so the user can see what the document
24	      is about at a glance.
25	- [ ] Common stopwords (the, a, is, of, etc.) never appear in the output,
26	      even when they are the most frequent raw tokens in the text.
27	- [ ] The number of words shown is configurable by the user, without
28	      editing the tool itself.
29	- [ ] The tool runs as a single standalone program with no import from or
30	      link against any other project's codebase, so it can be dropped into
31	      any environment and run on its own.
32	
33	## Known Requirements
34	
35	- **[HARD]** Standalone CLI with no dependency on any existing codebase —
36	  stated directly by the user ("Known: it is a standalone CLI with no
37	  dependency on any existing codebase"). This is a durable constraint on the
38	  tool itself, not an artifact of the sandbox being empty, so it should hold
39	  even if this code is later moved into a real repository.
40	- **[NEED]** Reads a UTF-8-encoded text file given as input.
41	- **[NEED]** Excludes a built-in list of common stopwords from the output.
42	- **[NEED]** The count of words displayed is configurable per run.
43	- **[INFERRED]** Word matching is case-insensitive ("The" and "the" count as
44	  the same word) — a case-sensitive count would fragment common words across
45	  capitalization variants and make the "most frequent" list less meaningful,
46	  which conflicts with the stated goal of an at-a-glance, meaningful result.
47	- **[INFERRED]** Tokenization must not corrupt or split accented/non-ASCII
48	  Latin characters (e.g. "café", "naïve") — a plain ASCII-only tokenizer
49	  would mangle these despite the tool accepting UTF-8 input. How far word
50	  handling extends past accented Latin text (Cyrillic, Greek, CJK, etc.) is
51	  not settled here — see Open Questions.
52	- **[INFERRED]** The stopword list is a built-in English list for v1 — the
53	  user's examples (the, a, is, of) are English, and no other language was
54	  mentioned.
55	- **[INFERRED]** Output is deterministic — running the tool twice on the
56	  same file produces the same ranked list in the same order, which is part
57	  of being a trustworthy at-a-glance reference. The specific rule for
58	  breaking frequency ties is a separate, unsettled design choice — see Open
59	  Questions.
60	
61	## Non-Goals
62	
63	- No GUI or interactive/REPL mode.
64	- No batch or multi-file processing in one invocation.
65	- No multi-language stopword lists or locale-specific tokenization.
66	- No persistence, history, or writing results to a file (stdout only).
67	- No charting or visualization of frequencies.
68	- Not intended for source code, logs, or other structured/repetitive text —
69	  frequency-minus-stopwords is a reasonable proxy for topic in prose, but not
70	  in code or logs, where the highest-frequency non-stopword tokens are often
71	  boilerplate (keywords, identifiers, timestamps) rather than topical
72	  content.
73	
74	## Open Questions / Deferred to design
75	
76	- **Implementation language/runtime** — not specified by the user. The
77	  requirement is language-agnostic, so this is a design choice.
78	- **CLI flag names/syntax** for the input file and the "number of words"
79	  option, and the default count used when the flag is omitted — a mechanism
80	  choice, not a stated requirement.
81	- **Tokenization edge cases** — contractions ("don't"), hyphenated
82	  compounds, embedded digits, and how far word handling should reach past
83	  accented Latin text (Cyrillic, Greek, CJK, etc., which may need
84	  locale-specific segmentation rather than simple letter-run matching).
85	  Affects both word boundaries and stopword matching; belongs to design.
86	- **Tie-break rule for equal-frequency words** — e.g. alphabetical,
87	  first-occurrence, or reverse-alphabetical order. Determinism itself is a
88	  requirement (see Known Requirements); which specific rule produces it is a
89	  design pick. Alphabetical is a reasonable default absent other guidance.
90	- **Stopword list customization** — whether a user can supply their own
91	  list/file in addition to the built-in default. Not requested; a plausible
92	  future extension.
93	- **Output format** — plain list vs. word/count columns vs. alignment.
94	  Cosmetic, belongs to design/UX.
95	- **Error handling** — behavior on missing file, invalid/non-UTF-8 encoding,
96	  empty file, or a file with zero non-stopword words. Deferred to design,
97	  but should fail with a clear message rather than a crash/stack trace.
98	
99	---
100	
101	## Related Artifacts
102	
103	- **Epic:** None
104	- **Research:** None
105	- **Design:** `.project/active/wordfreq/design.md` (to be created)
106	
107	---
108	
109	**Next Steps:** After approval, proceed to `/_my_design`.
110

file.content:
# Spec: wordfreq CLI

**Status:** Draft
**Owner:** Reid W
**Created:** 2026-07-02
**Complexity:** LOW
**Branch:** N/A (no commits yet in this sandbox)

---

## Problem

There's no quick way to see which words dominate a text document. Doing this
by hand, or with a one-off script each time, is repetitive, and a naive word
count is dominated by function words (the, a, is, of, ...) that carry no
information about what the document is actually about. A small CLI that
counts words, strips stopwords, and shows the top N gives an at-a-glance
read on any text file's content.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Running the tool against a text file prints a ranked list of the
      document's most frequent words, so the user can see what the document
      is about at a glance.
- [ ] Common stopwords (the, a, is, of, etc.) never appear in the output,
      even when they are the most frequent raw tokens in the text.
- [ ] The number of words shown is configurable by the user, without
      editing the tool itself.
- [ ] The tool runs as a single standalone program with no import from or
      link against any other project's codebase, so it can be dropped into
      any environment and run on its own.

## Known Requirements

- **[HARD]** Standalone CLI with no dependency on any existing codebase —
  stated directly by the user ("Known: it is a standalone CLI with no
  dependency on any existing codebase"). This is a durable constraint on the
  tool itself, not an artifact of the sandbox being empty, so it should hold
  even if this code is later moved into a real repository.
- **[NEED]** Reads a UTF-8-encoded text file given as input.
- **[NEED]** Excludes a built-in list of common stopwords from the output.
- **[NEED]** The count of words displayed is configurable per run.
- **[INFERRED]** Word matching is case-insensitive ("The" and "the" count as
  the same word) — a case-sensitive count would fragment common words across
  capitalization variants and make the "most frequent" list less meaningful,
  which conflicts with the stated goal of an at-a-glance, meaningful result.
- **[INFERRED]** Tokenization must not corrupt or split accented/non-ASCII
  Latin characters (e.g. "café", "naïve") — a plain ASCII-only tokenizer
  would mangle these despite the tool accepting UTF-8 input. How far word
  handling extends past accented Latin text (Cyrillic, Greek, CJK, etc.) is
  not settled here — see Open Questions.
- **[INFERRED]** The stopword list is a built-in English list for v1 — the
  user's examples (the, a, is, of) are English, and no other language was
  mentioned.
- **[INFERRED]** Output is deterministic — running the tool twice on the
  same file produces the same ranked list in the same order, which is part
  of being a trustworthy at-a-glance reference. The specific rule for
  breaking frequency ties is a separate, unsettled design choice — see Open
  Questions.

## Non-Goals

- No GUI or interactive/REPL mode.
- No batch or multi-file processing in one invocation.
- No multi-language stopword lists or locale-specific tokenization.
- No persistence, history, or writing results to a file (stdout only).
- No charting or visualization of frequencies.
- Not intended for source code, logs, or other structured/repetitive text —
  frequency-minus-stopwords is a reasonable proxy for topic in prose, but not
  in code or logs, where the highest-frequency non-stopword tokens are often
  boilerplate (keywords, identifiers, timestamps) rather than topical
  content.

## Open Questions / Deferred to design

- **Implementation language/runtime** — not specified by the user. The
  requirement is language-agnostic, so this is a design choice.
- **CLI flag names/syntax** for the input file and the "number of words"
  option, and the default count used when the flag is omitted — a mechanism
  choice, not a stated requirement.
- **Tokenization edge cases** — contractions ("don't"), hyphenated
  compounds, embedded digits, and how far word handling should reach past
  accented Latin text (Cyrillic, Greek, CJK, etc., which may need
  locale-specific segmentation rather than simple letter-run matching).
  Affects both word boundaries and stopword matching; belongs to design.
- **Tie-break rule for equal-frequency words** — e.g. alphabetical,
  first-occurrence, or reverse-alphabetical order. Determinism itself is a
  requirement (see Known Requirements); which specific rule produces it is a
  design pick. Alphabetical is a reasonable default absent other guidance.
- **Stopword list customization** — whether a user can supply their own
  list/file in addition to the built-in default. Not requested; a plausible
  future extension.
- **Output format** — plain list vs. word/count columns vs. alignment.
  Cosmetic, belongs to design/UX.
- **Error handling** — behavior on missing file, invalid/non-UTF-8 encoding,
  empty file, or a file with zero non-stopword words. Deferred to design,
  but should fail with a clear message rather than a crash/stack trace.

---

## Related Artifacts

- **Epic:** None
- **Research:** None
- **Design:** `.project/active/wordfreq/design.md` (to be created)

---

**Next Steps:** After approval, proceed to `/_my_design`.

content.content:
1	# Spec Review: wordfreq CLI
2	
3	**Spec:** `.project/active/wordfreq/spec.md`
4	**Contract:** `claude-pack/commands/_my_spec.md` (not readable from this sandboxed session — review conducted against the generation criteria embedded in the spec-review command itself: HARD/NEED/INFERRED tagging, section shape, and traceability)
5	**Review File:** `.project/active/wordfreq/spec-review.md`
6	**Date:** 2026-07-02
7	
8	---
9	
10	## Reality Check
11	
12	**Sound.** This is a small, self-contained spec for a standalone word-frequency CLI. There are no upstream artifacts to cross-check (Epic: None, Research: None), and the repo is empty (no code, no git history), so there's nothing in the codebase that could contradict a code-facing claim. The Problem section is coherent, the requirements plausibly trace back to it, and the scope (Non-Goals) is tight. This spec is pointed at the right work item.
13	
14	One limitation on this review's confidence: I have no access to the original conversation between the user and the spec agent, so claims like "stated directly as a boundary condition" can't be independently checked against the user's actual words. I've treated those as plausible but unverified rather than as confirmed facts — see L1-2.
15	
16	Proceeding to the full lens audit.
17	
18	---
19	
20	## Audit
21	
22	### Lens 1 — Faithfulness
23	
24	**L1-1 · Rewrite request:** The `[INFERRED]` tokenization item ("words are runs of letters/digits (not ASCII-only)") states a tokenization *mechanism*, not an outcome, and its scope is ambiguous. Read narrowly, it covers accented Latin text (café, naïve) — a solid inference from the UTF-8 requirement. Read broadly, "or non-Latin words" in the justification implies coverage of any script, including ones that don't tokenize on whitespace/letter runs at all (CJK). The spec can't have it both ways, and as written it doesn't say which. Rewrite the outcome as something like "accented and non-ASCII Latin characters must not be corrupted or split by tokenization," and push script-coverage scope (how far past Latin this goes) into the existing "Tokenization edge cases" Open Question rather than asserting it as settled.
25	
26	**L1-2 · Question to the user:** The `[HARD]` requirement "Standalone CLI with no dependency on any existing codebase" is annotated as "stated directly as a boundary condition." I can't verify that against the original conversation in this session. **Can you confirm you actually said this as a hard constraint, and not just "this happens to be a scratch project so there's nothing to depend on"?** Those are different claims — the first is a durable requirement, the second is incidental context that wouldn't survive if this code ever moved into a real codebase.
27	
28	### Lens 2 — Problem & Approach
29	
30	**L2-1 · Question to the user:** The spec's implicit bet is that "frequency count minus stopwords" is a good enough proxy for "what a document is about." That's a reasonable bet for prose (articles, notes, essays) but weak for structured or repetitive text — source code, logs, CSV dumps — where the highest-frequency non-stopword tokens are often boilerplate (`return`, variable names, timestamps) rather than topical content. **Is this tool meant only for prose-style text?** If so, it's worth one line in Non-Goals ("not intended for source code or structured/log data") so design and future users don't treat it as a general-purpose file summarizer.
31	
32	### Lens 3 — Pipeline Risk
33	
34	**L3-1 · Direct claim:** The `[INFERRED]` "ties broken alphabetically" item is a mechanism choice wearing an inference's clothes. The genuinely inferable need is *determinism* ("output must be stable and reproducible across runs on the same input") — that much follows from wanting a trustworthy at-a-glance tool. But "alphabetically" specifically is one of several ways to satisfy that (could just as easily be first-occurrence order, or reverse-alphabetical), and nothing in the Problem or the user's stated examples picks alphabetical over the alternatives. Recommend: keep "output must be deterministic" as the INFERRED requirement, and move the specific tie-break rule to Open Questions as a design pick (a sensible default, but not a requirement).
35	
36	**L3-2 · Contradiction:** The Unicode-tokenization item flagged in L1-1 sits in tension with the stated Non-Goal "No multi-language stopword lists or locale-specific tokenization." If tokenization is meant to handle "non-Latin words" broadly, that's arguably locale-specific tokenization by another name — different scripts need different word-boundary logic. As written, a downstream design agent could reasonably resolve this either way (Latin-only with diacritics, vs. any whitespace-delimited script), and get a different result each time. Resolving L1-1's scope question resolves this too.
37	
38	**L3-3 · Missing content:** No Success Criterion covers the `[HARD]` "standalone, no dependency on any existing codebase" requirement — nothing in the Success Criteria section would catch a violation of it. Given the sandbox has no other code to depend on, the risk is low today, but if this spec or its pattern gets reused in a real repo, the omission stops being harmless. Low priority given the current context; worth a one-line addition if this spec template gets reused elsewhere.
39	
40	### Lens 4 — Hygiene
41	
42	No material issues. Sections follow the expected shape, metadata (owner, date, complexity) is filled in and consistent with the sandbox context (branch "N/A" correctly reflects there being no git repo yet).
43	
44	### Lens 5 — Reader Comprehension
45	
46	No material issues. The spec is short, uses plain language throughout, and a reader can take in the whole thing in one pass. No jargon, no undefined terms, no buried decisions.
47	
48	---
49	
50	## Engagement Summary
51	
52	**Overall take:** This is a sound, low-complexity spec for the right work item — nothing here suggests Rework. The issues are all in the same family: two `[INFERRED]` items assert a specific mechanism (a tokenization rule, a tie-break rule) instead of the outcome that actually follows from the user's ask, and one of those mechanisms quietly contradicts a stated Non-Goal. These are quick edits, not a re-scope.
53	
54	**Here's what I need you to weigh in on:**
55	
56	1. **[L1-1, L3-2]** Decide how far tokenization should reach past accented Latin text (Cyrillic/Greek? CJK?), or explicitly punt the whole question to design — either resolves the contradiction with the "no locale-specific tokenization" Non-Goal.
57	2. **[L3-1]** Decide whether "alphabetical" tie-breaking is a real requirement or just a reasonable default — if the latter, it should move to Open Questions rather than sit tagged as settled.
58	3. **[L1-2]** Confirm the "standalone CLI, no codebase dependency" HARD requirement is something you actually said as a durable constraint, not just incidental to this being a scratch project.
59	4. **[L2-1]** Confirm whether this tool is meant only for prose-style text — if so, worth a one-line Non-Goal.
60	5. **[L3-3]** Optional, low priority: add a Success Criterion (or acceptance note) that would catch a violation of the standalone-CLI requirement, in case this spec pattern gets reused in a non-sandbox repo.
61	
62	---
63	
64	**Verdict:** Revise
65	**Next Steps:** Resolve items 1–4 above (item 5 is optional), tighten the two `[INFERRED]` items per L1-1/L3-1, then re-run `/_my_spec` to fold the edits in before proceeding to `/_my_design`.
66

# Spec Review: wordfreq CLI

**Spec:** `.project/active/wordfreq/spec.md`
**Contract:** `claude-pack/commands/_my_spec.md` (not readable from this sandboxed session — review conducted against the generation criteria embedded in the spec-review command itself: HARD/NEED/INFERRED tagging, section shape, and traceability)
**Review File:** `.project/active/wordfreq/spec-review.md`
**Date:** 2026-07-02

---

## Reality Check

**Sound.** This is a small, self-contained spec for a standalone word-frequency CLI. There are no upstream artifacts to cross-check (Epic: None, Research: None), and the repo is empty (no code, no git history), so there's nothing in the codebase that could contradict a code-facing claim. The Problem section is coherent, the requirements plausibly trace back to it, and the scope (Non-Goals) is tight. This spec is pointed at the right work item.

One limitation on this review's confidence: I have no access to the original conversation between the user and the spec agent, so claims like "stated directly as a boundary condition" can't be independently checked against the user's actual words. I've treated those as plausible but unverified rather than as confirmed facts — see L1-2.

Proceeding to the full lens audit.

---

## Audit

### Lens 1 — Faithfulness

**L1-1 · Rewrite request:** The `[INFERRED]` tokenization item ("words are runs of letters/digits (not ASCII-only)") states a tokenization *mechanism*, not an outcome, and its scope is ambiguous. Read narrowly, it covers accented Latin text (café, naïve) — a solid inference from the UTF-8 requirement. Read broadly, "or non-Latin words" in the justification implies coverage of any script, including ones that don't tokenize on whitespace/letter runs at all (CJK). The spec can't have it both ways, and as written it doesn't say which. Rewrite the outcome as something like "accented and non-ASCII Latin characters must not be corrupted or split by tokenization," and push script-coverage scope (how far past Latin this goes) into the existing "Tokenization edge cases" Open Question rather than asserting it as settled.

**L1-2 · Question to the user:** The `[HARD]` requirement "Standalone CLI with no dependency on any existing codebase" is annotated as "stated directly as a boundary condition." I can't verify that against the original conversation in this session. **Can you confirm you actually said this as a hard constraint, and not just "this happens to be a scratch project so there's nothing to depend on"?** Those are different claims — the first is a durable requirement, the second is incidental context that wouldn't survive if this code ever moved into a real codebase.

### Lens 2 — Problem & Approach

**L2-1 · Question to the user:** The spec's implicit bet is that "frequency count minus stopwords" is a good enough proxy for "what a document is about." That's a reasonable bet for prose (articles, notes, essays) but weak for structured or repetitive text — source code, logs, CSV dumps — where the highest-frequency non-stopword tokens are often boilerplate (`return`, variable names, timestamps) rather than topical content. **Is this tool meant only for prose-style text?** If so, it's worth one line in Non-Goals ("not intended for source code or structured/log data") so design and future users don't treat it as a general-purpose file summarizer.

### Lens 3 — Pipeline Risk

**L3-1 · Direct claim:** The `[INFERRED]` "ties broken alphabetically" item is a mechanism choice wearing an inference's clothes. The genuinely inferable need is *determinism* ("output must be stable and reproducible across runs on the same input") — that much follows from wanting a trustworthy at-a-glance tool. But "alphabetically" specifically is one of several ways to satisfy that (could just as easily be first-occurrence order, or reverse-alphabetical), and nothing in the Problem or the user's stated examples picks alphabetical over the alternatives. Recommend: keep "output must be deterministic" as the INFERRED requirement, and move the specific tie-break rule to Open Questions as a design pick (a sensible default, but not a requirement).

**L3-2 · Contradiction:** The Unicode-tokenization item flagged in L1-1 sits in tension with the stated Non-Goal "No multi-language stopword lists or locale-specific tokenization." If tokenization is meant to handle "non-Latin words" broadly, that's arguably locale-specific tokenization by another name — different scripts need different word-boundary logic. As written, a downstream design agent could reasonably resolve this either way (Latin-only with diacritics, vs. any whitespace-delimited script), and get a different result each time. Resolving L1-1's scope question resolves this too.

**L3-3 · Missing content:** No Success Criterion covers the `[HARD]` "standalone, no dependency on any existing codebase" requirement — nothing in the Success Criteria section would catch a violation of it. Given the sandbox has no other code to depend on, the risk is low today, but if this spec or its pattern gets reused in a real repo, the omission stops being harmless. Low priority given the current context; worth a one-line addition if this spec template gets reused elsewhere.

### Lens 4 — Hygiene

No material issues. Sections follow the expected shape, metadata (owner, date, complexity) is filled in and consistent with the sandbox context (branch "N/A" correctly reflects there being no git repo yet).

### Lens 5 — Reader Comprehension

No material issues. The spec is short, uses plain language throughout, and a reader can take in the whole thing in one pass. No jargon, no undefined terms, no buried decisions.

---

## Engagement Summary

**Overall take:** This is a sound, low-complexity spec for the right work item — nothing here suggests Rework. The issues are all in the same family: two `[INFERRED]` items assert a specific mechanism (a tokenization rule, a tie-break rule) instead of the outcome that actually follows from the user's ask, and one of those mechanisms quietly contradicts a stated Non-Goal. These are quick edits, not a re-scope.

**Here's what I need you to weigh in on:**

1. **[L1-1, L3-2]** Decide how far tokenization should reach past accented Latin text (Cyrillic/Greek? CJK?), or explicitly punt the whole question to design — either resolves the contradiction with the "no locale-specific tokenization" Non-Goal.
2. **[L3-1]** Decide whether "alphabetical" tie-breaking is a real requirement or just a reasonable default — if the latter, it should move to Open Questions rather than sit tagged as settled.
3. **[L1-2]** Confirm the "standalone CLI, no codebase dependency" HARD requirement is something you actually said as a durable constraint, not just incidental to this being a scratch project.
4. **[L2-1]** Confirm whether this tool is meant only for prose-style text — if so, worth a one-line Non-Goal.
5. **[L3-3]** Optional, low priority: add a Success Criterion (or acceptance note) that would catch a violation of the standalone-CLI requirement, in case this spec pattern gets reused in a non-sandbox repo.

---

**Verdict:** Revise
**Next Steps:** Resolve items 1–4 above (item 5 is optional), tighten the two `[INFERRED]` items per L1-1/L3-1, then re-run `/_my_spec` to fold the edits in before proceeding to `/_my_design`.

# Spec Review: wordfreq CLI

**Spec:** `.project/active/wordfreq/spec.md`
**Contract:** `claude-pack/commands/_my_spec.md` (not present in this sandboxed session — no `claude-pack` directory exists in either allowed working directory. Reviewed against the generation criteria embedded in the spec-review command itself: HARD/NEED/INFERRED tagging, section shape, and traceability.)
**Review File:** `.project/active/wordfreq/spec-review.md`
**Date:** 2026-07-02

---

## Reality Check

**Sound.** This spec has already been revised once — a prior `spec-review.md` on disk shows an earlier round that recommended **Revise**, and the current spec.md incorporates that round's fixes almost line-for-line: the tokenization `[INFERRED]` item is now scoped to accented Latin text with script-coverage pushed to Open Questions, the tie-break rule moved out of the determinism requirement into Open Questions, a "not intended for source code or logs" Non-Goal was added, and a Success Criterion for the standalone-CLI constraint now exists. That round's fixes hold up under a fresh check — no new contradiction was introduced by them.

This is a small, self-contained spec (Epic: None, Research: None, no existing code to contradict) for the right work item. The Problem section is coherent and the requirements trace back to it. Proceeding to a fresh full-lens pass rather than just re-checking the prior round's items, since a new review should stand on its own.

---

## Audit

### Lens 1 — Faithfulness

**L1-1 · Question to the user:** The `[HARD]` tag on "standalone CLI, no dependency on any existing codebase" is sourced to a quote — `"Known: it is a standalone CLI with no dependency on any existing codebase"` — that reads like a line from a task brief, not a spoken sentence. The prior review round asked whether this was really a durable constraint or just incidental to the sandbox being empty; the spec answered by *adding an assertion* ("This is a durable constraint on the tool itself... should hold even if this code is later moved into a real repository") rather than by citing anything new from the user. That's the spec agent resolving its own question, not the user resolving it. **Is the durability of this constraint actually something you said, or is the added sentence the spec agent's best guess?** If it's a guess, it should read as `[INFERRED]`, not `[HARD]` — `[HARD]` is reserved for things forced by the user's actual words.

**L1-2 · Question to the user:** "Reads a UTF-8-encoded text file given as input" is tagged `[NEED]` — a stakeholder outcome the user asked for — but as written it names an encoding *mechanism*, not an outcome. The outcome the user plausibly cares about is "the tool works on the text files I actually have." UTF-8 is a reasonable default for that outcome on any modern system, which is the shape of an `[INFERRED]` item, not a `[NEED]`. **Did you specifically say "UTF-8," or is that the spec agent's default?** If the latter, retag it `[INFERRED]` — it doesn't change what gets built, but it changes whether a reviewer can trust the `[NEED]` tags as "the user asked for this specifically."

### Lens 2 — Problem & Approach

No new findings. The prior round's gap here — the spec's implicit bet that frequency-minus-stopwords is a good "what's this document about" proxy, which is weak for code/log-style text — is now covered by the added Non-Goal ("Not intended for source code, logs, or other structured/repetitive text"). That Non-Goal is consistent with the Problem section's framing (`document`, `prose`-shaped examples) and doesn't introduce any new tension.

### Lens 3 — Pipeline Risk

**L3-1 · Rewrite request:** The "Error handling" Open Question reads: *"behavior on missing file, invalid/non-UTF-8 encoding, empty file, or a file with zero non-stopword words. Deferred to design, but should fail with a clear message rather than a crash/stack trace."* The clause "should fail with a clear message rather than a crash/stack trace" is not a design-stage mechanism question — it's a `[NEED]`-shaped outcome (the tool doesn't dump a stack trace on bad input) sitting unprotected inside a deferred item. Nothing in Known Requirements or Success Criteria would catch a violation of it today; a design that lets a missing file throw a raw traceback would satisfy every requirement currently on the page. Split this: promote "must fail with a clear, non-crash message on bad input" to Known Requirements (a genuine `[NEED]`) with a matching Success Criterion, and leave only the *specific wording/format* of error messages and *exact list of error conditions to handle* in Open Questions.

**L3-2 · Missing content:** Neither the requirements nor the Open Questions address whether morphological variants of a word ("run," "running," "ran") should be merged into one bucket or counted separately. This isn't cosmetic — it changes the actual ranked output. Two engineers building this in good faith could reasonably diverge: one treats each surface form as a distinct word (simplest reading of "counts words"), the other stems/lemmatizes toward the Problem section's real goal ("see what the document is about"). As written, the spec is silent, so this would be decided ad hoc in design rather than as a scoped choice. Recommend adding it to Open Questions — e.g., "Stemming/lemmatization: are `run`/`running`/`ran` one word or three? Not requested; default should be exact-token matching (case-insensitive) unless design finds a reason otherwise" — so it's a visible, deliberate deferral rather than a silent gap.

### Lens 4 — Hygiene

No material issues. Section shape, metadata, and the "no git repo yet" branch note are all consistent with the actual sandbox state.

### Lens 5 — Reader Comprehension

No material issues. The spec stays short and plain through both this round and the prior revision; no jargon or buried decisions.

---

## Engagement Summary

**Overall take:** The prior review round's fixes landed cleanly and didn't introduce new problems — this spec is close. What's left is one real completeness gap (error handling) and one silent design ambiguity (morphological variants) that could produce visibly different tools depending on who builds it, plus two lower-stakes tag-honesty questions left over from the last round that got resolved by assertion rather than by confirmation.

**Here's what I need you to weigh in on:**

1. **[L3-1]** Decide whether "must not crash with a raw stack trace on bad input" is a real requirement (recommend: yes) — if so, it needs to move out of Open Questions into Known Requirements with a matching Success Criterion, since nothing currently enforces it.
2. **[L3-2]** Decide whether word-form variants (run/running/ran) should be merged or counted separately, or explicitly punt it to design as a named Open Question — right now it's an unscoped gap, not a deliberate deferral.
3. **[L1-1]** Confirm the standalone-CLI constraint's *durability* (survives a move out of this sandbox) is something you actually said, not an inference the spec agent added to answer its own question from the last round.
4. **[L1-2]** Confirm whether "UTF-8" was your actual requirement or a reasonable default the spec agent filled in — affects whether it should be tagged `[NEED]` or `[INFERRED]`.

---

**Verdict:** Revise
**Next Steps:** Resolve items 1–2 (real gaps, not just tag hygiene) and ideally 3–4 (tag accuracy), then re-run `/_my_spec` to fold the edits in before proceeding to `/_my_design`.

lines: # Spec Review: wordfreq CLI
**Spec:** `.project/active/wordfreq/spec.md`

-**Contract:** `claude-pack/commands/_my_spec.md` (not readable from this sandboxed session — review conducted against the generation criteria embedded in the spec-review command itself: HARD/NEED/INFERRED tagging, section shape, and traceability)

+**Contract:** `claude-pack/commands/_my_spec.md` (not present in this sandboxed session — no `claude-pack` directory exists in either allowed working directory. Reviewed against the generation criteria embedded in the spec-review command itself: HARD/NEED/INFERRED tagging, section shape, and traceability.)
**Review File:** `.project/active/wordfreq/spec-review.md`
## Reality Check

-**Sound.** This is a small, self-contained spec for a standalone word-frequency CLI. There are no upstream artifacts to cross-check (Epic: None, Research: None), and the repo is empty (no code, no git history), so there's nothing in the codebase that could contradict a code-facing claim. The Problem section is coherent, the requirements plausibly trace back to it, and the scope (Non-Goals) is tight. This spec is pointed at the right work item.

+**Sound.** This spec has already been revised once — a prior `spec-review.md` on disk shows an earlier round that recommended **Revise**, and the current spec.md incorporates that round's fixes almost line-for-line: the tokenization `[INFERRED]` item is now scoped to accented Latin text with script-coverage pushed to Open Questions, the tie-break rule moved out of the determinism requirement into Open Questions, a "not intended for source code or logs" Non-Goal was added, and a Success Criterion for the standalone-CLI constraint now exists. That round's fixes hold up under a fresh check — no new contradiction was introduced by them.

-One limitation on this review's confidence: I have no access to the original conversation between the user and the spec agent, so claims like "stated directly as a boundary condition" can't be independently checked against the user's actual words. I've treated those as plausible but unverified rather than as confirmed facts — see L1-2.

+This is a small, self-contained spec (Epic: None, Research: None, no existing code to contradict) for the right work item. The Problem section is coherent and the requirements trace back to it. Proceeding to a fresh full-lens pass rather than just re-checking the prior round's items, since a new review should stand on its own.
-Proceeding to the full lens audit.
## Audit
### Lens 1 — Faithfulness

-**L1-1 · Rewrite request:** The `[INFERRED]` tokenization item ("words are runs of letters/digits (not ASCII-only)") states a tokenization *mechanism*, not an outcome, and its scope is ambiguous. Read narrowly, it covers accented Latin text (café, naïve) — a solid inference from the UTF-8 requirement. Read broadly, "or non-Latin words" in the justification implies coverage of any script, including ones that don't tokenize on whitespace/letter runs at all (CJK). The spec can't have it both ways, and as written it doesn't say which. Rewrite the outcome as something like "accented and non-ASCII Latin characters must not be corrupted or split by tokenization," and push script-coverage scope (how far past Latin this goes) into the existing "Tokenization edge cases" Open Question rather than asserting it as settled.

+**L1-1 · Question to the user:** The `[HARD]` tag on "standalone CLI, no dependency on any existing codebase" is sourced to a quote — `"Known: it is a standalone CLI with no dependency on any existing codebase"` — that reads like a line from a task brief, not a spoken sentence. The prior review round asked whether this was really a durable constraint or just incidental to the sandbox being empty; the spec answered by *adding an assertion* ("This is a durable constraint on the tool itself... should hold even if this code is later moved into a real repository") rather than by citing anything new from the user. That's the spec agent resolving its own question, not the user resolving it. **Is the durability of this constraint actually something you said, or is the added sentence the spec agent's best guess?** If it's a guess, it should read as `[INFERRED]`, not `[HARD]` — `[HARD]` is reserved for things forced by the user's actual words.

-**L1-2 · Question to the user:** The `[HARD]` requirement "Standalone CLI with no dependency on any existing codebase" is annotated as "stated directly as a boundary condition." I can't verify that against the original conversation in this session. **Can you confirm you actually said this as a hard constraint, and not just "this happens to be a scratch project so there's nothing to depend on"?** Those are different claims — the first is a durable requirement, the second is incidental context that wouldn't survive if this code ever moved into a real codebase.

+**L1-2 · Question to the user:** "Reads a UTF-8-encoded text file given as input" is tagged `[NEED]` — a stakeholder outcome the user asked for — but as written it names an encoding *mechanism*, not an outcome. The outcome the user plausibly cares about is "the tool works on the text files I actually have." UTF-8 is a reasonable default for that outcome on any modern system, which is the shape of an `[INFERRED]` item, not a `[NEED]`. **Did you specifically say "UTF-8," or is that the spec agent's default?** If the latter, retag it `[INFERRED]` — it doesn't change what gets built, but it changes whether a reviewer can trust the `[NEED]` tags as "the user asked for this specifically."
### Lens 2 — Problem & Approach

-**L2-1 · Question to the user:** The spec's implicit bet is that "frequency count minus stopwords" is a good enough proxy for "what a document is about." That's a reasonable bet for prose (articles, notes, essays) but weak for structured or repetitive text — source code, logs, CSV dumps — where the highest-frequency non-stopword tokens are often boilerplate (`return`, variable names, timestamps) rather than topical content. **Is this tool meant only for prose-style text?** If so, it's worth one line in Non-Goals ("not intended for source code or structured/log data") so design and future users don't treat it as a general-purpose file summarizer.

+No new findings. The prior round's gap here — the spec's implicit bet that frequency-minus-stopwords is a good "what's this document about" proxy, which is weak for code/log-style text — is now covered by the added Non-Goal ("Not intended for source code, logs, or other structured/repetitive text"). That Non-Goal is consistent with the Problem section's framing (`document`, `prose`-shaped examples) and doesn't introduce any new tension.
### Lens 3 — Pipeline Risk

-**L3-1 · Direct claim:** The `[INFERRED]` "ties broken alphabetically" item is a mechanism choice wearing an inference's clothes. The genuinely inferable need is *determinism* ("output must be stable and reproducible across runs on the same input") — that much follows from wanting a trustworthy at-a-glance tool. But "alphabetically" specifically is one of several ways to satisfy that (could just as easily be first-occurrence order, or reverse-alphabetical), and nothing in the Problem or the user's stated examples picks alphabetical over the alternatives. Recommend: keep "output must be deterministic" as the INFERRED requirement, and move the specific tie-break rule to Open Questions as a design pick (a sensible default, but not a requirement).

+**L3-1 · Rewrite request:** The "Error handling" Open Question reads: *"behavior on missing file, invalid/non-UTF-8 encoding, empty file, or a file with zero non-stopword words. Deferred to design, but should fail with a clear message rather than a crash/stack trace."* The clause "should fail with a clear message rather than a crash/stack trace" is not a design-stage mechanism question — it's a `[NEED]`-shaped outcome (the tool doesn't dump a stack trace on bad input) sitting unprotected inside a deferred item. Nothing in Known Requirements or Success Criteria would catch a violation of it today; a design that lets a missing file throw a raw traceback would satisfy every requirement currently on the page. Split this: promote "must fail with a clear, non-crash message on bad input" to Known Requirements (a genuine `[NEED]`) with a matching Success Criterion, and leave only the *specific wording/format* of error messages and *exact list of error conditions to handle* in Open Questions.

-**L3-2 · Contradiction:** The Unicode-tokenization item flagged in L1-1 sits in tension with the stated Non-Goal "No multi-language stopword lists or locale-specific tokenization." If tokenization is meant to handle "non-Latin words" broadly, that's arguably locale-specific tokenization by another name — different scripts need different word-boundary logic. As written, a downstream design agent could reasonably resolve this either way (Latin-only with diacritics, vs. any whitespace-delimited script), and get a different result each time. Resolving L1-1's scope question resolves this too.

+**L3-2 · Missing content:** Neither the requirements nor the Open Questions address whether morphological variants of a word ("run," "running," "ran") should be merged into one bucket or counted separately. This isn't cosmetic — it changes the actual ranked output. Two engineers building this in good faith could reasonably diverge: one treats each surface form as a distinct word (simplest reading of "counts words"), the other stems/lemmatizes toward the Problem section's real goal ("see what the document is about"). As written, the spec is silent, so this would be decided ad hoc in design rather than as a scoped choice. Recommend adding it to Open Questions — e.g., "Stemming/lemmatization: are `run`/`running`/`ran` one word or three? Not requested; default should be exact-token matching (case-insensitive) unless design finds a reason otherwise" — so it's a visible, deliberate deferral rather than a silent gap.

-**L3-3 · Missing content:** No Success Criterion covers the `[HARD]` "standalone, no dependency on any existing codebase" requirement — nothing in the Success Criteria section would catch a violation of it. Given the sandbox has no other code to depend on, the risk is low today, but if this spec or its pattern gets reused in a real repo, the omission stops being harmless. Low priority given the current context; worth a one-line addition if this spec template gets reused elsewhere.
### Lens 4 — Hygiene

-No material issues. Sections follow the expected shape, metadata (owner, date, complexity) is filled in and consistent with the sandbox context (branch "N/A" correctly reflects there being no git repo yet).
+No material issues. Section shape, metadata, and the "no git repo yet" branch note are all consistent with the actual sandbox state.
### Lens 5 — Reader Comprehension
-No material issues. The spec is short, uses plain language throughout, and a reader can take in the whole thing in one pass. No jargon, no undefined terms, no buried decisions.
+No material issues. The spec stays short and plain through both this round and the prior revision; no jargon or buried decisions.
## Engagement Summary

-**Overall take:** This is a sound, low-complexity spec for the right work item — nothing here suggests Rework. The issues are all in the same family: two `[INFERRED]` items assert a specific mechanism (a tokenization rule, a tie-break rule) instead of the outcome that actually follows from the user's ask, and one of those mechanisms quietly contradicts a stated Non-Goal. These are quick edits, not a re-scope.

+**Overall take:** The prior review round's fixes landed cleanly and didn't introduce new problems — this spec is close. What's left is one real completeness gap (error handling) and one silent design ambiguity (morphological variants) that could produce visibly different tools depending on who builds it, plus two lower-stakes tag-honesty questions left over from the last round that got resolved by assertion rather than by confirmation.
**Here's what I need you to weigh in on:**

-1. **[L1-1, L3-2]** Decide how far tokenization should reach past accented Latin text (Cyrillic/Greek? CJK?), or explicitly punt the whole question to design — either resolves the contradiction with the "no locale-specific tokenization" Non-Goal.
-2. **[L3-1]** Decide whether "alphabetical" tie-breaking is a real requirement or just a reasonable default — if the latter, it should move to Open Questions rather than sit tagged as settled.
-3. **[L1-2]** Confirm the "standalone CLI, no codebase dependency" HARD requirement is something you actually said as a durable constraint, not just incidental to this being a scratch project.
-4. **[L2-1]** Confirm whether this tool is meant only for prose-style text — if so, worth a one-line Non-Goal.

-5. **[L3-3]** Optional, low priority: add a Success Criterion (or acceptance note) that would catch a violation of the standalone-CLI requirement, in case this spec pattern gets reused in a non-sandbox repo.

+1. **[L3-1]** Decide whether "must not crash with a raw stack trace on bad input" is a real requirement (recommend: yes) — if so, it needs to move out of Open Questions into Known Requirements with a matching Success Criterion, since nothing currently enforces it.

+2. **[L3-2]** Decide whether word-form variants (run/running/ran) should be merged or counted separately, or explicitly punt it to design as a named Open Question — right now it's an unscoped gap, not a deliberate deferral.

+3. **[L1-1]** Confirm the standalone-CLI constraint's *durability* (survives a move out of this sandbox) is something you actually said, not an inference the spec agent added to answer its own question from the last round.
+4. **[L1-2]** Confirm whether "UTF-8" was your actual requirement or a reasonable default the spec agent filled in — affects whether it should be tagged `[NEED]` or `[INFERRED]`.
**Verdict:** Revise
-**Next Steps:** Resolve items 1–4 above (item 5 is optional), tighten the two `[INFERRED]` items per L1-1/L3-1, then re-run `/_my_spec` to fold the edits in before proceeding to `/_my_design`.
+**Next Steps:** Resolve items 1–2 (real gaps, not just tag hygiene) and ideally 3–4 (tag accuracy), then re-run `/_my_spec` to fold the edits in before proceeding to `/_my_design`.

### Lens 2 — Problem & Approach

**L2-1 · Question to the user:** The spec's implicit bet is that "frequency count minus stopwords" is a good enough proxy for "what a document is about." That's a reasonable bet for prose (articles, notes, essays) but weak for structured or repetitive text — source code, logs, CSV dumps — where the highest-frequency non-stopword tokens are often boilerplate (`return`, variable names, timestamps) rather than topical content. **Is this tool meant only for prose-style text?** If so, it's worth one line in Non-Goals ("not intended for source code or structured/log data") so design and future users don't treat it as a general-purpose file summarizer.

### Lens 3 — Pipeline Risk

**L3-1 · Direct claim:** The `[INFERRED]` "ties broken alphabetically" item is a mechanism choice wearing an inference's clothes. The genuinely inferable need is *determinism* ("output must be stable and reproducible across runs on the same input") — that much follows from wanting a trustworthy at-a-glance tool. But "alphabetically" specifically is one of several ways to satisfy that (could just as easily be first-occurrence order, or reverse-alphabetical), and nothing in the Problem or the user's stated examples picks alphabetical over the alternatives. Recommend: keep "output must be deterministic" as the INFERRED requirement, and move the specific tie-break rule to Open Questions as a design pick (a sensible default, but not a requirement).

**L3-2 · Contradiction:** The Unicode-tokenization item flagged in L1-1 sits in tension with the stated Non-Goal "No multi-language stopword lists or locale-specific tokenization." If tokenization is meant to handle "non-Latin words" broadly, that's arguably locale-specific tokenization by another name — different scripts need different word-boundary logic. As written, a downstream design agent could reasonably resolve this either way (Latin-only with diacritics, vs. any whitespace-delimited script), and get a different result each time. Resolving L1-1's scope question resolves this too.

**L3-3 · Missing content:** No Success Criterion covers the `[HARD]` "standalone, no dependency on any existing codebase" requirement — nothing in the Success Criteria section would catch a violation of it. Given the sandbox has no other code to depend on, the risk is low today, but if this spec or its pattern gets reused in a real repo, the omission stops being harmless. Low priority given the current context; worth a one-line addition if this spec template gets reused elsewhere.

### Lens 4 — Hygiene

No material issues. Sections follow the expected shape, metadata (owner, date, complexity) is filled in and consistent with the sandbox context (branch "N/A" correctly reflects there being no git repo yet).

### Lens 5 — Reader Comprehension

No material issues. The spec is short, uses plain language throughout, and a reader can take in the whole thing in one pass. No jargon, no undefined terms, no buried decisions.

---

## Engagement Summary

**Overall take:** This is a sound, low-complexity spec for the right work item — nothing here suggests Rework. The issues are all in the same family: two `[INFERRED]` items assert a specific mechanism (a tokenization rule, a tie-break rule) instead of the outcome that actually follows from the user's ask, and one of those mechanisms quietly contradicts a stated Non-Goal. These are quick edits, not a re-scope.

**Here's what I need you to weigh in on:**

1. **[L1-1, L3-2]** Decide how far tokenization should reach past accented Latin text (Cyrillic/Greek? CJK?), or explicitly punt the whole question to design — either resolves the contradiction with the "no locale-specific tokenization" Non-Goal.
2. **[L3-1]** Decide whether "alphabetical" tie-breaking is a real requirement or just a reasonable default — if the latter, it should move to Open Questions rather than sit tagged as settled.
3. **[L1-2]** Confirm the "standalone CLI, no codebase dependency" HARD requirement is something you actually said as a durable constraint, not just incidental to this being a scratch project.
4. **[L2-1]** Confirm whether this tool is meant only for prose-style text — if so, worth a one-line Non-Goal.
5. **[L3-3]** Optional, low priority: add a Success Criterion (or acceptance note) that would catch a violation of the standalone-CLI requirement, in case this spec pattern gets reused in a non-sandbox repo.

---

**Verdict:** Revise
**Next Steps:** Resolve items 1–4 above (item 5 is optional), tighten the two `[INFERRED]` items per L1-1/L3-1, then re-run `/_my_spec` to fold the edits in before proceeding to `/_my_design`.

My pass found two remaining issues worth acting on and two lower-stakes tag questions, written up in the new `spec-review.md`.

VERDICT: Revise
